rooms.name: The Monarch Theatre — Grand Lobby
rooms.description: The grand lobby of the Monarch Theatre. A dusty chandelier hangs above cracked marble floors. Faded playbills line the walls, and a brass ticket booth sits dark and empty. The air smells of old velvet and forgotten glamour.

hotspots.label: Concierge Desk
hotspots.description: A curved concierge desk with a guest ledger. A master key sits on a brass hook behind it. There's a note in shaky handwriting: 'Nancy — take the key. V.D.'

hotspots.label: Wall of Playbills
hotspots.description: Dozens of playbills spanning decades. One is framed in gold: 'THE CRIMSON VEIL — Starring Margaux Fontaine — Final Performance, October 31, 1928.' Someone has placed fresh flowers beneath it.

hotspots.label: Chandelier
hotspots.description: A massive crystal chandelier, dark and dust-covered. A few prisms still catch the light from the lobby windows, casting faint rainbows on the marble floor.

hotspots.label: Auditorium Doors
hotspots.description: The ornate double doors swing open with a groan, revealing the vast darkness of the auditorium beyond.

hotspots.label: Faded Poster
hotspots.description: A half-torn touring poster: 'BLACKMOOR PLAYERS present: The Penvellyn Curse — A Tale of Alchemy and Shadows.' Someone's written in pencil at the bottom: 'Brilliant. Almost too real. —M.F.' Margaux Fontaine saw this show.

rooms.name: The Monarch Theatre — Auditorium
rooms.description: A vast auditorium stretches before you. Rows of velvet seats descend toward a grand stage, its crimson curtain drawn shut. The air is thick and still. A single ghost light burns center stage.

hotspots.label: The Stage
hotspots.description: The stage where Margaux Fontaine collapsed during the final act of The Crimson Veil, October 31, 1928. A single bare bulb — the ghost light — stands at center stage. Theater tradition says it keeps ghosts away. Or invites them.

hotspots.label: Audience Seats
hotspots.description: Rows of dusty velvet seats. You check the seat pockets — mostly old ticket stubs. One pocket holds a faded playbill from the 1928 performance.

hotspots.label: Crimson Curtain
hotspots.description: The original crimson velvet curtain, heavy with age. Gold tassels hang from its edges. A small brass plaque at stage left reads: 'For Margaux — the last light of the Monarch.'

rooms.name: The Monarch Theatre — Backstage
rooms.description: A labyrinth of rigging ropes, costume racks, and stacked set pieces. The air smells of sawdust and old paint. Somewhere in the shadows, something mechanical hums.

hotspots.label: Fog Machine
hotspots.description: An industrial fog machine, old but functional. The fluid reservoir is half-empty and the nozzle is still damp. Someone used this recently.

hotspots.label: Technical Shelf
hotspots.description: Shelves of old stage manuals and equipment logs. One binder stands out: 'MONARCH THEATRE — SPECIAL EFFECTS MANUAL, 1925.' It details fog machines, trapdoors, and hidden passages built into the original construction.

hotspots.label: Prop Table
hotspots.description: A cluttered prop table. There's a dusty globe, some trick daggers, and a small stone gargoyle that looks like it belongs in an English manor. A tag reads: 'Blackmoor Manor set — 1932 touring production. DO NOT DISCARD.' Strange — it feels heavier than a prop should.

hotspots.label: Costume Rack
hotspots.description: Rows of costumes from past productions. One white gown near the back seems out of place — it's not dusty at all. The hem is damp.

hotspots.label: Basement Door
hotspots.description: The heavy door swings open, revealing a steep staircase descending into darkness beneath the stage.

rooms.name: Margaux's Dressing Room
rooms.description: Time stopped here in 1928. A vanity mirror surrounded by lightbulbs — half of them dead — reflects dusty perfume bottles and a dried corsage. A velvet settee sits beneath a rack of moth-eaten costumes. The room smells of old powder and roses.

hotspots.label: Margaux's Vanity
hotspots.description: A vanity table with a cracked mirror surrounded by lightbulbs. Margaux's name is still painted on the mirror in faded gold. The surface holds dried roses, an empty perfume bottle, and a hairbrush with dark strands still caught in the bristles.

hotspots.label: Vanity Drawer
hotspots.description: Inside the drawer: a leather-bound diary with M.F. embossed in gold on the cover.

hotspots.label: Locked Trunk
hotspots.description: An antique trunk with a combination lock. Three dials, each numbered 0–9.

hotspots.label: Wall Photos
hotspots.description: Framed photographs of Margaux in various roles. In one, she stands with a younger woman — the inscription reads 'With my dear Cecilia, who will one day outshine us all.' The glass over Cecilia's face is cracked.

hotspots.label: Old Book
hotspots.description: A leather-bound volume wedged behind the settee cushion: 'The Alchemist's Apprentice — A Study of Poisons and Their Remedies, 1890.' Several pages are dog-eared. Margaux was doing her own research into what was happening to her.

rooms.name: Projection Booth
rooms.description: A cramped booth above the auditorium, crammed with old projectors and film canisters. Diego Reyes has set up a makeshift writing desk between the equipment. Papers and coffee cups everywhere.

hotspots.label: Old Projector
hotspots.description: A 1920s carbon arc projector, beautifully preserved. A film reel is still loaded — the label reads 'The Crimson Veil — Rehearsal Footage, 1928.' The film is too degraded to play.

hotspots.label: Annotated Script
hotspots.description: The original script of 'The Crimson Veil' with handwritten annotations in the margins. The notes are in some kind of code — letters circled in red ink throughout the stage directions.

hotspots.label: Booth Window
hotspots.description: Through the thick glass, you can see the entire auditorium below — the stage, the seats, the balcony. A perfect vantage point. From here, you'd see everything that happened on that stage.

rooms.name: Manager's Office
rooms.description: Roland Ashworth's temporary headquarters. A modern laptop sits incongruously on an ornate mahogany desk. Demolition blueprints are pinned to the walls. A cot in the corner suggests he's been sleeping here. He looks pale and unwell.

hotspots.label: Blueprints
hotspots.description: Demolition blueprints for the Monarch Theatre. But someone has drawn over them in red pen — marking hidden passages, sealed rooms, and what looks like a tunnel network beneath the stage. These aren't Ashworth's notes.

hotspots.label: Teacup
hotspots.description: A porcelain teacup with dried residue at the bottom. Whatever Ashworth drank, the remains are still here.

hotspots.label: Laptop
hotspots.description: Ashworth's laptop, open to an email chain. The latest message: 'The historical society's petition is meaningless. Proceed with demolition on schedule. — R.A.' A reply draft reads: 'There's someone in the building at night. I have proof.'

hotspots.label: Cot
hotspots.description: A camp cot with rumpled blankets. On the pillow: a small flashlight and a book — 'Ghosts of the American Theatre.' A chapter on the Monarch is bookmarked.

rooms.name: The Catwalk
rooms.description: A narrow metal walkway suspended above the stage. Lighting rigs hang at eye level, and the stage floor is a dizzying distance below. The ghost light flickers far beneath you. Every step echoes.

hotspots.label: Lighting Rig
hotspots.description: A bank of theatrical spotlights, some original from the 1920s. One spotlight has been recently adjusted — its gel filter is crimson red. It's aimed directly at center stage, exactly where Margaux would have stood.

hotspots.label: Leather Notebook
hotspots.description: A leather notebook tucked behind a lighting fixture. The first page reads: 'The Ghost Project — E.H.' Pages of research, timelines, diagrams of the theater's hidden passage system.

hotspots.label: Look Down
hotspots.description: The stage stretches out far below. From up here, you can see scuff marks on the stage floor — a path worn between the trapdoor and stage left. Someone's been using that trapdoor regularly.

hotspots.label: Fly System
hotspots.description: The theater's original counterweight fly system — ropes and pulleys for raising and lowering scenery. One line has been recently re-rigged. Whatever it lifts, it's been used within the last few days.

rooms.name: Beneath the Stage
rooms.description: A cavernous space beneath the stage. Massive trapdoor mechanisms loom in the shadows. Old set pieces lean against the walls like sleeping giants. In the far corner, a work lamp illuminates a makeshift workshop that wasn't here in 1928.

hotspots.label: Workshop
hotspots.description: Edwin's hidden workshop. A table covered in historical documents, a fog machine remote control, a white costume on a dress form, and chemical bottles. This is where the ghost was made.

hotspots.label: Fog Machine Controls
hotspots.description: A remote control system wired to the fog machines upstairs. It's been modified for one-person operation — someone could trigger fog, lights, and the trapdoor from right here.

hotspots.label: Trapdoor Mechanism
hotspots.description: The original stage trapdoor mechanism. Recently oiled and fully functional. A pulley system can lift a person from the basement to center stage in seconds. This is how the 'ghost' appeared and vanished.

hotspots.label: Brick Alcove
hotspots.description: A section of the basement wall has been carefully dismantled, revealing a hidden alcove. Inside: a bundle of letters tied with ribbon — correspondence between Cecilia Drake and a pharmacist, dated 1928. This is the proof that Margaux was murdered.
